This paper presents a novel approach to optimizing transformer architectures through
knowledge distillation and quantization. We evaluate on standard benchmarks including
GLUE and SuperGLUE, demonstrating competitive performance with significantly reduced
model size. Our method leverages structured pruning combined with post-training
quantization to achieve a 4x compression ratio while retaining 98% of the original
model's accuracy.

We conduct experiments on three major model families and compare against existing
compression baselines including structured magnitude pruning, unstructured pruning,
and standard knowledge distillation. Our approach consistently outperforms these
baselines on downstream task performance while achieving faster inference times.

The key insight is that attention heads in large language models exhibit significant
redundancy, particularly in the middle layers. By selectively removing redundant
attention heads and applying layer-wise quantization calibrated on a small calibration
set, we achieve impressive compression with minimal performance degradation.

We additionally demonstrate that our compressed models generalize well to new domains
not seen during calibration, which is an important practical consideration for
deployment in real-world applications. The compressed models maintain strong
few-shot learning capabilities despite the significant parameter reduction.

Future work will explore applying our compression framework to multimodal architectures
and investigating combinations with speculative decoding for further inference speedups.
We release code and compressed model checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility.
